title: Spontaneous symmetry breaking and Goldstone modes for deep information propagation
authors: Nabil Iqbal, T. Anderson Keller, Yue Song, Takeru Miyato, Max Welling
abstract: In physical systems, whenever a continuous symmetry is spontaneously broken, the system possesses excitations called Goldstone modes, which allow coherent information propagation over long distances and times. In this work, we study deep neural networks whose internal layers are equivariant under a continuous symmetry and may therefore support analogous Goldstone-like degrees of freedom. We demonstrate, both analytically and empirically, that these degrees of freedom enable coherent signal propagation across depth and recurrent iterations, providing a mechanism for stable information flow without relying on architectural stabilizers such as residual connections or normalization. In feedforward networks, this results in improved trainability and representational diversity across layers. In recurrent settings, we demonstrate the same mechanism is valuable for long-term memory by propagating information over recurrent iterations, thereby improving performance of RNNs and GRUs on long-sequence modeling tasks.
summary: This paper proposes using the physics of spontaneous symmetry breaking as a mechanism for information propagation in neural networks. By using equivariant architectures, the authors show that 'Goldstone-like' modes protect signal integrity across many layers or time steps, effectively replacing the need for architectural stabilizers like skip connections.
main_problem: Investigating how spontaneous symmetry breaking and the resulting Goldstone modes can enable stable, long-distance information propagation and prevent signal collapse in deep and recurrent neural networks.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that in the symmetry-broken phase, equivariant networks possess a protected component of the Jacobian that remains O(1) regardless of depth, enabling improved trainability and long-term memory without traditional stabilizers like residual connections.
method: The study uses mean-field theory, stochastic path integrals, and the replica method in the large-N limit to analytically derive activation dynamics, complemented by empirical testing on tasks like Fashion-MNIST and variable-delay copy tasks.
model_or_system: The models consist of deep feedforward and recurrent neural networks (RNNs, GRUs) with internal layers that are equivariant under continuous symmetry groups such as U(1) or O(k).
key_observables: Order parameter (c_l), Jacobian magnitude and its protected component, effective rank of representations, and vorticity/winding numbers in 2D convolutional models.
important_parameters: Weight variance (sigma_W), symmetry dimension (k), network depth (L), and recurrent delay length (T).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the large-N limit for mean-field derivations and that the tasks being performed do not necessarily possess the underlying symmetry.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the potential energy landscape of symmetry breaking, the phase transition in activation variance, the stability of the Jacobian component in the SSB phase, and the emergence of topological vortices in 2D convolutional RNNs.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical analogy of Goldstone modes, provides an analytical mean-field derivation of the symmetry-breaking phase transition, presents empirical evidence for improved information propagation in deep and recurrent architectures, and concludes with observations of topological defects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Equilibrium configurations are minima of the potential energy V (ϕ). If r > 0 we have a single isolated minimum at ϕ = 0 and the symmetry is unbroken. If r < 0 we find a ring of minima with constant |ϕ|, of which the system must choose one; the symmetry is spontaneously broken.
Figure 2: Comparison of mean-field result for c⋆

Figure 3: Test accuracy after 5 epochs on Fashion-MNIST for U(1) (left) and O(4) equivariant (right) models with 64 features and 100 layers. We compare against models with the same nonlinearity but generic linear layers, thus isolating the effect of the equivariance. We vary σw to probe the symmetry breaking phase transition, normalizing it so that the transition is always at σw = 1. The training of the equivariant models appears to begin precisely when we enter the SSB phase, as measured by the order parameter c⋆, which we compute at initialization from (6) averaged over the last 30 layers. Training of the non-equivariant models is poor, as expected for deep networks with no mitigation.
Figure 5: Effective rank dynamics during training (left) and across network depth (right).

Figure 4: Demonstration of how performance on Fashion-MNIST degrades with increasing layer number for a generic network and for different sorts of equivari- ance. Note the O(4) model does not degrade at all.
